front: What are the four phases of the Unified Process (UP)?
back: Inception (feasibility, scope), Elaboration (core architecture, risk resolution), Construction (building the product), Transition (deployment to user community).

front: What are the six best practices that form the foundation of the Unified Process?
back: 1. Develop software iteratively, 2. Manage requirements, 3. Use component-based architecture, 4. Visually model software (UML), 5. Continuously verify software quality, 6. Control changes to software.

front: What is the difference between a workflow and a phase in the Unified Process?
back: A phase (e.g., Inception) is a time span in the project's life cycle. A workflow (e.g., Requirements) is a set of activities that produce a specific result and can occur across different phases.

front: What are the nine disciplines (formerly workflows) in a UP project?
back: Business Modeling, Requirements, Analysis & Design, Implementation, Test, Deployment, Configuration & Change Management, Project Management, Environment.

front: What core principle distinguishes the Unified Process from a linear waterfall model?
back: It is iterative and incremental. Development is organized into a series of short, fixed-length iterations (e.g., 2-4 weeks), each resulting in an executable increment of the software, allowing for frequent feedback and adaptation.
